name: Stockholm
summary: Stockholm sits on 14 islands stitched together by more than 50 bridges, so people call it the Venice of the North. You can step into Gamla Stan, a medieval old town from the 1250s, peek at a giant 400-year-old warship, and visit the homes of Pippi Longstocking, ABBA, and the Nobel Prizes.
funFacts: Stockholm is built on 14 islands joined by over 50 bridges, and the wider archipelago has around 30,000 islands and tiny rocky islets.
The huge warship Vasa sank just 1,300 metres into its very first voyage in 1628, then was raised whole from the seabed in 1961.
Pippi Longstocking author Astrid Lindgren and the pop group ABBA both came from Sweden, and Stockholm has special museums for each.
Stockholm's Royal Palace has more than 600 rooms across 11 floors, making it one of the biggest working royal palaces in Europe.
Most Nobel Prizes are handed out in Stockholm every December 10, the day inventor Alfred Nobel died, inside the famous City Hall.
thingsToDo: Visit the Vasa Museum on Djurgården island to see the giant 17th-century warship that sank, sat underwater 333 years, and was rescued.
Wander Gamla Stan's cobbled lanes, then watch the Changing of the Royal Guard at the Royal Palace courtyard, usually at 12:15pm.
Explore Skansen, the world's oldest open-air museum, and meet Nordic animals like brown bears, moose, lynx, reindeer, and wolverines.
Hop aboard Junibacken's Story Train, narrated by Astrid Lindgren herself, and end the ride at Pippi Longstocking's upside-down Villa Villekulla.
Sing, dance and try on virtual costumes with hologram band members at ABBA The Museum, right next door to Gröna Lund.
Ride classic coasters and the 1892 carousel at Gröna Lund, Sweden's oldest amusement park, on the Djurgården waterfront.

images.alt: Bright orange, red and yellow old houses leaning together around Stortorget square in Stockholm's Old Town.
The huge wooden Vasa warship, almost 400 years old, towering inside the Vasa Museum in Stockholm.
Stockholm City Hall with its tall red brick tower rising beside the water, where the Nobel Prize banquet is held.
The grand pale-yellow Royal Palace of Stockholm, home of the Swedish king and queen.
Drottningholm Palace, a beautiful royal palace by the lake just outside Stockholm with neat green gardens.
